---
title: "Pupil tracking (poster)"
---

A two-panel poster figure for the `black` (dark surround) rotating-mouse
eye-tracking dataset: the four tracked keypoints (plus the pupil centroid) on an
example video frame, and the pupil centroid's horizontal velocity over time.

## Imports and style

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import sleap_io as sio

from movement import sample_data
from movement.kinematics import compute_velocity

from movement_figures import apply_style, figure_size, get_color, save_figure

apply_style("poster")
```

## Load and prepare data

We use a dataset that's already part of `movement`'s sample data and has been
thoroughly explored in `movement`'s ['Pupil tracking' example](https://movement.neuroinformatics.dev/latest/examples/mouse_eye_movements.html).


```{python}
# Load the black (dark surround) eye-tracking dataset together with its video.
ds = sample_data.fetch_dataset(
    "DLC_rotating-mouse_eye-tracking_stim-black.predictions.h5",
    with_video=True,
)
frame0 = sio.load_video(ds.video_path)[0]

# Panel 1 data: keypoints at the first frame and the pupil centroid.
pos0 = ds.position.isel(time=0).squeeze()
centroid0 = pos0.sel(keypoint=["pupil-L", "pupil-R"]).mean("keypoint")

# Panel 2 data: x-velocity of the normalized pupil centroid over time.
eye_mid = ds.position.sel(keypoint=["eye-L", "eye-R"]).mean("keypoint")
pos_norm = ds.position - eye_mid
pupil_c = pos_norm.sel(keypoint=["pupil-L", "pupil-R"]).mean("keypoint")
velocity = compute_velocity(pupil_c)
vx = velocity.sel(space="x").squeeze().sel(time=slice(10, 25))
```

## Build the figure

```{python}
fig = plt.figure(figsize=figure_size("double", height=3))
gs = fig.add_gridspec(
    1, 2,
    width_ratios=[5, 11],
    wspace=0.25,
    left=0.1, right=0.95, top=0.9, bottom=0.1,
)
ax_frame = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 0])  # example frame
ax_vel = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 1])    # x-velocity trace

# Panel 1 (~1/4 width): example frame with tracked keypoints + derived centroid.
ax_frame.imshow(frame0, cmap="gray")
# All 4 tracked keypoints share one color and one label -> a single legend entry.
h_tracked = ax_frame.scatter(
    pos0.sel(space="x"),
    pos0.sel(space="y"),
    s=120,
    color=get_color("teal", "Set2"),
)
h_centroid = ax_frame.scatter(
    centroid0.sel(space="x"),
    centroid0.sel(space="y"),
    s=120,
    color=get_color("orange", "Set2"),
)
ax_frame.invert_yaxis()  # the dataset was captured flipped

# Hide ticks and spines but keep the axis labels
ax_frame.set_xticks([])
ax_frame.set_yticks([])
for spine in ax_frame.spines.values():
    spine.set_visible(False)

ax_frame.set_xlabel("x")
ax_frame.set_ylabel("y")

# Panel 2 (~3/4 width): x-velocity of the normalized pupil centroid over time.
vx.plot.line(
    ax=ax_vel, color=get_color("orange", "Set2"), label="Pupil centroid x velocity"
)
ax_vel.set_xlim(float(vx.time.min()), float(vx.time.max()))
ax_vel.set_title("Pupil centroid x velocity")
ax_vel.set_xlabel("time (s)")
ax_vel.set_ylabel("velocity (px/s)");
```

## Save the figure

```{python}
fig_name = "pupil_tracking_poster"
save_paths = save_figure(fig, fig_name, formats=("pdf", "svg"))
print(f"Saved '{fig_name}' figure to {save_paths[0].parent}.")
```
